title: How to Protect Your Home Network from Hackers: A 2026 Security Guide
content:
# How to Protect Your Home Network from Hackers: A 2026 Security Guide

*Affiliate disclosure: This article contains affiliate links. If you purchase through our links, we may earn a commission at no extra cost to you. This does not influence our recommendations.*

**Last updated: March 3, 2026**

## Quick Answer: 5 Things You Should Do RIGHT NOW

If you only have 10 minutes, do these five things today — they block over 80% of common home network attacks:

1. **Change your router's default admin password** — 20% of IoT devices still use factory credentials, and routers are the #1 target
2. **Enable WPA3 encryption** on your WiFi (or WPA2-AES if your router doesn't support WPA3)
3. **Disable WPS, UPnP, and remote management** in your router settings — all three are known attack vectors
4. **Update your router's firmware** — 33% of IoT devices globally run outdated firmware with known exploitable flaws
5. **Use a unique password for every device and account** — get a password manager like [Bitwarden](https://bitwarden.com/) (free) or [1Password](https://1password.com/) ($2.99/mo)

For full network hardening, follow all 10 steps below. Each step includes specific tools, costs, and difficulty level so you know exactly what you're doing.

**Related articles:** [Best VPN Services 2026](/software/best-vpn-services-2026) | [Best Password Managers 2026](/software/best-password-managers-2026) | [Best Antivirus Software 2026](/software/best-antivirus-software-2026) | [Best Smart Home Devices 2026](/home/best-smart-home-devices-2026)

---

## Why This Matters: Home Networks Are Under Attack

The numbers in 2026 are staggering — and they're not theoretical risks. This is happening to real households, every single day.

**The threat landscape:**

- **Over 2,200 cyberattacks occur every day globally** — that's roughly one attack every 39 seconds, according to Cybersecurity Ventures
- **Smart home cyber attacks have surged to 29 attempts per household daily** in 2026, with 38% of smart home IoT devices having been compromised at least once
- **The average American household now contains 22 connected devices** — smart speakers, thermostats, security cameras, refrigerators, doorbells, and more. Each one is a potential entry point
- **67% of smart home devices ship with at least one critical vulnerability**, and 80% of IoT devices remain vulnerable to a wide range of attacks
- **IoT malware incidents increased 124%** from late 2024 to late 2025, with AI-driven IoT attacks surging an additional 54% in early 2026
- **Global cybercrime damages reached $10.29 trillion** in 2025, with ransomware accounting for 68% of all detected threats
- **91% of all cyberattacks begin with a phishing email**, and approximately 3.4 billion phishing emails are sent worldwide every day
- **AI-powered phishing scams increased 400%** due to generative AI tools that create messages indistinguishable from legitimate communications

Your home router is the front door to everything — your bank accounts, your work files, your family photos, your kids' devices. If someone compromises your router, they can see every packet of data flowing through your network. The FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center (IC3) reported that 3.26 million complaints were filed over the last five years, with losses totaling $27.6 billion.

The good news: most home network attacks exploit **basic misconfigurations** that you can fix in an afternoon. Here's exactly how.

---

## Step 1: Secure Your Router (Difficulty: Easy | Cost: Free | Time: 15 min)

Your router is the single most important security device in your home. Every device connects through it. If it's compromised, everything else is too.

### What to do:

**Change the default admin credentials.** Every router ships with a factory username and password (usually "admin/admin" or "admin/password"). Attackers know these defaults. Log into your router's admin panel (typically at 192.168.1.1 or 192.168.0.1) and change both the username and password to something unique.

**Update your router's firmware immediately.** Manufacturers regularly release patches for security vulnerabilities. Check your router's admin panel for a firmware update option, or visit the manufacturer's website. According to IoT security research, 33% of IoT devices globally run outdated firmware containing known, exploitable security flaws. Set a reminder to check for updates quarterly.

**Disable WPS (Wi-Fi Protected Setup).** WPS was designed for convenience — press a button, connect a device. But it has a well-known brute-force vulnerability that attackers can exploit in hours. Turn it off in your router settings.

**Disable UPnP (Universal Plug and Play).** UPnP lets devices automatically open ports on your router, which is convenient for gaming consoles and smart TVs but creates security holes. Disable it and manually configure port forwarding only for devices that truly need it.

**Disable remote management.** Unless you specifically need to access your router from outside your home network (you almost certainly don't), turn off remote management/remote access in your router settings.

**Change the default DNS settings.** Most routers default to your ISP's DNS servers, which may log your browsing history. Switch to a privacy-focused DNS provider like Cloudflare (1.1.1.1) or Quad9 (9.9.9.9) for faster, more private resolution. More on this in Step 4.

### Recommended routers with strong security:

| Router | WiFi Standard | Security Features | Price |
|--------|--------------|-------------------|-------|
| ASUS RT-AX86U Pro | WiFi 6 | AiProtection Pro (Trend Micro), automatic firmware updates, VPN server built-in | ~$230 |
| Netgear Nighthawk RAXE500 | WiFi 6E | Netgear Armor (Bitdefender), automatic threat detection, guest network | ~$350 |
| TP-Link Archer AXE75 | WiFi 6E | HomeShield security suite, real-time IoT protection, parental controls | ~$180 |
| GL.iNet Flint 2 | WiFi 6 | OpenWrt-based, built-in VPN client/server, AdGuard Home compatible | ~$100 |

---

## Step 2: Set Up a Strong WiFi Configuration (Difficulty: Easy | Cost: Free | Time: 10 min)

### Enable WPA3 encryption

WPA3 is the current gold standard for WiFi security. It offers strong protection against brute-force attacks and has built-in protections for weak passwords through its Simultaneous Authentication of Equals (SAE) handshake. Switch your wireless security mode to WPA3 in your router settings.

If your router doesn't support WPA3, use WPA2-AES (not WPA2-TKIP, which has known vulnerabilities). If your router only supports WPA or WEP, it's time to replace it — those protocols can be cracked in minutes.

**Important WPA3 caveat:** Researchers have identified vulnerabilities in WPA3 implementations, including the "Dragonblood" attacks that can leak information during the handshake process. Additionally, mixed-mode networks that enable both WPA2 and WPA3 create a security loophole — attackers can force WPA3-capable clients to fall back to the more vulnerable WPA2. For maximum security, use WPA3-only mode if all your devices support it.

### Create a guest network

Set up a separate guest WiFi network for visitors. This prevents guests' potentially infected devices from accessing your main network, your shared files, and your smart home devices. Most modern routers support this — look for "Guest Network" in your router settings.

### Consider hiding your SSID

Hiding your network name (SSID) won't stop a determined attacker — network scanning tools can still detect hidden networks. But it does prevent casual drive-by discovery and keeps your network out of the list that neighbors see. It's one additional friction point.

### Use a strong WiFi password

Your WiFi password should be at least 16 characters, combining uppercase, lowercase, numbers, and symbols. Better yet, use a passphrase: "CoffeeTable$Running4Sharks!" is both strong and memorable.

---

## Step 3: Use a VPN at the Router Level (Difficulty: Medium | Cost: $2-5/mo | Time: 30 min)

Installing a VPN on your router protects every device on your network simultaneously — including devices that don't support VPN apps natively, like smart TVs, gaming consoles, and IoT sensors.

### Why router-level VPN matters:

- Encrypts ALL traffic leaving your network, not just traffic from one device
- Prevents your ISP from logging your browsing history (which they can legally sell in the US)
- Protects devices that can't run VPN software (smart home devices, IoT sensors, older gadgets)
- Uses a single VPN connection slot to protect 22+ devices simultaneously

### Recommended VPNs for router installation:

| VPN | Price (2-year) | Router Support | Speed Loss | Key Feature |
|-----|---------------|---------------|------------|-------------|
| [NordVPN](https://nordvpn.com/) | $3.39/mo | ASUS, Netgear, TP-Link, GL.iNet, OpenWrt | 4.4% | Threat Protection Pro blocks malware at DNS level |
| [ExpressVPN](https://expressvpn.com/) | $2.44/mo | Custom firmware (Aircove router), many brands | 18% | Easiest router setup with custom firmware |
| [Surfshark](https://surfshark.com/) | $1.99/mo | ASUS, DD-WRT, OpenWrt, Tomato | 11% | Unlimited devices, lowest price |
| [Proton VPN](https://protonvpn.com/) | $2.99/mo | OpenVPN/WireGuard compatible routers | 8% | Swiss privacy laws, open-source, free tier available |

**Pro tip:** If you don't want to configure VPN on your existing router, consider the [ExpressVPN Aircove](https://expressvpn.com/) ($190) or a [GL.iNet travel router](https://www.gl-inet.com/) ($30-100) — both come with VPN built in. The GL.iNet Flint 2 supports WireGuard natively and can connect to any VPN provider.

For a deeper comparison of all major providers, see our full guide: [Best VPN Services 2026](/software/best-vpn-services-2026).

---

## Step 4: Enable DNS-Level Protection (Difficulty: Easy-Medium | Cost: Free-$20/yr | Time: 15 min)

DNS-level blocking stops malicious websites, ads, trackers, and phishing domains before they load — across your entire network, on every device, without installing software on each one.

### The three best options:

**NextDNS (Recommended for most people)** — Works like Pi-hole but in the cloud. No hardware needed. Point your router's DNS to NextDNS servers and you get ad blocking, tracker blocking, malware protection, and detailed analytics. Free tier includes 300,000 queries/month; Pro is $1.99/month or $19.90/year for unlimited queries.

**Pi-hole (Best for self-hosted control)** — Install on a Raspberry Pi ($35-50) or any Linux machine. Acts as your network's DNS server and blocks requests to known ad, tracker, and malware domains. Completely free software. Requires a bit more technical setup but gives you full control over your blocklists. Pairs well with Cloudflare DNS-over-HTTPS for encrypted DNS queries.

**Cloudflare 1.1.1.1 for Families (Simplest free option)** — Cloudflare offers two free DNS addresses that block threats automatically:
- `1.1.1.2` — blocks malware domains
- `1.1.1.3` — blocks malware AND adult content

Just change your router's DNS settings to these addresses and you get basic protection in under 2 minutes. No account needed.

**Quad9 (9.9.9.9)** is another free option that blocks known malicious domains using threat intelligence feeds from 25+ cybersecurity organizations. Quad9 was ranked among the best encrypted DNS services alongside NextDNS and Cloudflare in 2026 comparisons.

---

## Step 5: Secure IoT Devices (Difficulty: Medium | Cost: Free | Time: 30 min)

IoT devices are the weakest link in most home networks. They often run minimal operating systems with limited (or no) security updates, and many phone home to servers you've never heard of.

### Critical IoT security steps:

**Put IoT devices on a separate network.** Create a dedicated WiFi network (most routers support multiple SSIDs) or VLAN for your smart home devices. This way, even if a smart lightbulb is compromised, the attacker can't reach your laptop or phone. This is the single most effective thing you can do for IoT security.

**Change default passwords on every device.** Smart cameras, doorbells, thermostats, speakers — every single one. Research shows that 20% of IoT devices still operate with factory default credentials. Use your password manager to generate and store unique passwords.

**Disable features you don't use.** If your smart TV has a microphone you never use, disable it. If your smart speaker doesn't need to make purchases, turn off voice purchasing. Every enabled feature is an attack surface.

**Update firmware regularly.** Check for firmware updates on all IoT devices monthly. Many manufacturers release critical security patches that fix known vulnerabilities. If a device no longer receives updates from its manufacturer, consider replacing it — an unpatched device is a permanent security hole.

**Research before you buy.** Before adding a new smart device to your home, check whether the manufacturer has a track record of releasing security updates, how data is stored (locally vs. cloud), and whether the device works without internet access (local-only devices are inherently more secure).

---

## Step 6: Use a Password Manager (Difficulty: Easy | Cost: Free-$3/mo | Time: 20 min)

The average person has 100+ online accounts. Using the same password across multiple accounts means that one data breach compromises everything. A password manager generates, stores, and auto-fills unique passwords for every account.

### Top password managers in 2026:

| Manager | Price | Free Tier | Key Advantage |
|---------|-------|-----------|---------------|
| [Bitwarden](https://bitwarden.com/) | $1.65/mo | Yes (unlimited passwords, multi-device sync) | Open-source, self-hostable, best free plan |
| [1Password](https://1password.com/) | $2.99/mo | No | Best interface, Privacy Cards for secure payments |
| [Dashlane](https://dashlane.com/) | ~$5/mo | Limited | Built-in VPN + dark web monitoring |
| [NordPass](https://nordpass.com/) | $1.49/mo | Yes (1 device) | Integrates with NordVPN ecosystem |

**Bitwarden is the best choice for most people** — it's open-source (code is publicly auditable), offers unlimited password storage and multi-device sync for free, and the premium tier at $1.65/month is less than half the price of 1Password. Independent security audits confirm AES-256 encryption with zero-knowledge architecture.

For our full comparison with pros, cons, and detailed testing: [Best Password Managers 2026](/software/best-password-managers-2026).

---

## Step 7: Enable Two-Factor Authentication Everywhere (Difficulty: Easy | Cost: Free | Time: 30 min)

Two-factor authentication (2FA) ensures that even if someone steals your password, they still can't access your account without a second verification factor. In 2026, only 52% of internet users have 2FA enabled on at least one account — which means nearly half the population is one leaked password away from disaster.

### Types of 2FA (ranked by security):

1. **Hardware security keys** (YubiKey, Google Titan) — most secure, phishing-resistant. A physical USB or NFC key that must be present to log in. ~$25-50 per key.
2. **Passkeys** — the emerging standard. Passkey authentications have doubled year-over-year, reaching 1.3 million per month globally. Google reports passkeys were used more than 1 billion times across 400+ million accounts. 38% of the top 100 websites now support passkeys.
3. **Authenticator apps** (Google Authenticator, Authy, Microsoft Authenticator) — generates time-based one-time codes on your phone. Free and widely supported.
4. **SMS codes** — better than nothing, but vulnerable to SIM-swapping attacks. Use only when no other option is available.

### Priority accounts for 2FA:

- Email (your email is the recovery method for everything else — if compromised, everything falls)
- Banking and financial accounts
- Cloud storage (Google Drive, iCloud, Dropbox)
- Social media accounts
- Your router's admin panel (if supported)
- Amazon, shopping accounts with saved payment methods
- Password manager (protects the keys to everything)

---

## Step 8: Set Up Network Monitoring (Difficulty: Medium | Cost: Free-$39/yr | Time: 15 min)

You can't protect what you can't see. Network monitoring tools alert you when unknown devices join your network, when traffic patterns change suspiciously, or when a device starts communicating with known malicious servers.

### Recommended monitoring tools:

**Fing (Free — iOS, Android)** — The simplest option for home users. Scans your WiFi, lists every connected device with manufacturer identification, and alerts you when a new device appears. Device catalog includes thousands of brands and models. Free for home use.

**GlassWire (Free / Pro $39/yr — Windows, Android)** — A modern personal firewall and network monitor with over 51 million downloads. Features intrusion detection via suspicious traffic analysis, system file change detection, ARP spoofing monitoring, and alerts when your computer communicates with known threat IPs. The free version covers basic monitoring; Pro adds historical data and advanced alerts.

**Bitdefender BOX or ASUS AiProtection** — If you have a compatible router (ASUS models with AiProtection, or Bitdefender's BOX hardware), these provide network-level threat detection, vulnerability assessment, and IoT protection without additional software.

**Wireshark (Free — Windows, macOS, Linux)** — For advanced users. Captures and analyzes every data packet on your network in real-time. Essential for security forensics and diagnosing unusual network behavior, but requires technical knowledge to interpret results.

### What to watch for:

- Unknown devices on your network (potential unauthorized access)
- Unusual outbound traffic from IoT devices (could indicate compromise)
- DNS queries to suspicious domains (malware phoning home)
- Significant bandwidth usage at unusual hours (data exfiltration)

---

## Step 9: Install Antivirus/Anti-Malware on All Devices (Difficulty: Easy | Cost: Free-$50/yr | Time: 15 min)

Network-level protection is critical, but endpoint protection on each device adds a second layer of defense. If malware gets past your router, antivirus software on your computer or phone is the last line of defense.

### Quick recommendations:

- **Best overall:** [Bitdefender Total Security](https://bitdefender.com/) — $19.99/yr for 3 devices, perfect 18/18 AV-TEST score, lightest system impact
- **Most complete:** [Norton 360 Deluxe](https://norton.com/) — $49.99/yr for 5 devices, includes unlimited VPN + 50GB cloud backup + dark web monitoring
- **Best free:** Windows Defender — 17.5/18 AV-TEST score, built into Windows 11, genuinely sufficient for careful users
- **Best second-opinion scanner:** [Malwarebytes](https://malwarebytes.com/) — runs alongside your primary antivirus to catch behavioral threats

For full reviews with pros, cons, and independent lab scores for 7 options: [Best Antivirus Software 2026](/software/best-antivirus-software-2026).

---

## Step 10: Regular Security Audits — The Monthly Checklist (Difficulty: Easy | Cost: Free | Time: 30 min/month)

Security is not a one-time setup. Threats evolve, new devices join your network, and firmware updates are released. Dedicate 30 minutes once a month to this checklist:

### Monthly security audit:

- [ ] **Scan for unknown devices** — Open Fing or your router's admin panel and verify every connected device. Remove anything you don't recognize
- [ ] **Check for firmware updates** — Router, smart cameras, doorbells, speakers, thermostats. Update anything with a pending patch
- [ ] **Review your router's connected device list** — Look for devices connecting at unusual times
- [ ] **Run a full antivirus scan** on all computers
- [ ] **Check your email in HaveIBeenPwned.com** — see if any of your accounts appeared in recent data breaches
- [ ] **Review app permissions on your phone** — revoke access for apps that don't need camera, microphone, or location
- [ ] **Test your VPN connection** — verify it's still active and your IP is masked (visit whatismyipaddress.com)
- [ ] **Review and update passwords** for any accounts flagged in breach notifications

### Quarterly tasks:

- [ ] Change your WiFi password
- [ ] Review and update your router's firmware
- [ ] Audit your password manager for weak, reused, or old passwords
- [ ] Check if any IoT devices have reached end-of-life (no longer receiving updates)
- [ ] Review your DNS-level blocking logs for suspicious patterns

---

## Essential Security Tools: Complete Reference

| Category | Free Option | Paid Option | What It Does |

| Breach Monitoring | [HaveIBeenPwned](https://haveibeenpwned.com/) | [Norton Dark Web Monitoring](https://norton.com/) | Alerts when credentials appear in breaches |

---

## Common Mistakes: What Most People Get Wrong

### Mistake #1: "My network is too small to be a target"
Attackers don't target specific households — they scan millions of IP addresses using automated tools, looking for routers with default credentials, open ports, or outdated firmware. Your home network is scanned whether you realize it or not. The 29 daily attack attempts per household stat proves it.

### Mistake #2: Relying on a single security measure
A VPN alone won't save you if your router password is "admin." Antivirus alone won't help if you reuse the same password on 50 accounts. Security is layered — no single tool is sufficient. This guide walks through 10 layers for a reason.

### Mistake #3: Never updating firmware
The firmware update process feels tedious, but it closes known security vulnerabilities. Manufacturers can't force you to update (unlike phone apps), so it's your responsibility. A router running 3-year-old firmware might have dozens of known exploits.

### Mistake #4: Using WPA2 mixed mode with WPA3
Mixed mode (WPA2+WPA3) exists for backward compatibility with older devices, but it creates a security downgrade attack vector. An attacker can force your WPA3-capable device to fall back to WPA2, which is weaker. If possible, use WPA3-only mode.

### Mistake #5: Putting everything on one network
Your work laptop, your kids' tablets, your smart doorbell, and your guest's phone should NOT all be on the same network. A compromised smart lightbulb on the same network as your banking laptop is a direct path to your financial data. Use network segmentation (guest network + IoT network + trusted device network).

### Mistake #6: Thinking Mac or iPhone users don't need protection
MacOS malware detections increased 27% year-over-year in 2025. iPhones can be phished just as easily as Android phones. No platform is immune. Cross-platform protection matters.

### Mistake #7: Ignoring physical security
If someone can physically access your router, they can reset it, plug in a USB device, or access the admin panel. Keep your router in a secured location, not in an unlocked apartment hallway or accessible garage.

---

## Frequently Asked Questions

### How do I know if my home network has been hacked?
Common signs include: unexplained slowdowns in internet speed, unknown devices appearing on your router's connected device list, browser redirects to unfamiliar websites, changed DNS settings on your router, unexplained data usage spikes, and security software being disabled without your action. Use a network scanner like Fing to audit all connected devices regularly.

### Is WPA3 enough to keep hackers out of my WiFi?
WPA3 is the most secure WiFi encryption available and protects against brute-force attacks, but it is not invulnerable. Researchers have demonstrated "Dragonblood" vulnerabilities in WPA3 implementations, and mixed WPA2/WPA3 mode creates downgrade attack opportunities. Use WPA3-only mode when possible, and combine WiFi encryption with the other layers described in this guide.

### Do I really need a VPN at home?
A VPN at home encrypts your traffic from your ISP (which can legally sell your browsing data in the US), protects against man-in-the-middle attacks, and secures IoT devices that can't run security software. It's not strictly necessary for basic browsing, but it adds significant privacy and security — especially at the router level where it protects all 22+ devices in your household.

### What's the cheapest way to secure my home network?
Completely free: change router defaults, enable WPA3, disable WPS/UPnP, use Windows Defender, install Bitwarden (free tier), set up 2FA with Google Authenticator, change DNS to Cloudflare 1.1.1.3, and use Fing for network monitoring. Total cost: $0. This covers about 85% of what you need.

### How often should I change my WiFi password?
Change your WiFi password quarterly, or immediately if you suspect unauthorized access, when a household member moves out, after a repair person or guest has had access, or if you've shared the password widely. Always use a strong, unique password stored in your password manager.

### Should I hide my WiFi network name (SSID)?
Hiding your SSID provides minimal security benefit — attackers with basic network scanning tools can still detect hidden networks. However, it does reduce casual discovery by neighbors and opportunistic attackers. Consider it one small friction point in your overall defense, not a primary security measure.

### Are smart home devices safe to use?
Smart home devices can be used safely with proper precautions: place them on a separate network (VLAN or guest WiFi), change default passwords, keep firmware updated, and disable unnecessary features like microphones and remote access. The biggest risk comes from devices that no longer receive manufacturer updates — replace any end-of-life smart device.

### What should I do if I think my network has been compromised?
Immediately: disconnect from the internet (unplug router). Then: factory reset your router, set new admin credentials, change your WiFi password, update firmware, scan all devices with antivirus software, change passwords on critical accounts (email, banking) from a different network (mobile data), enable 2FA on all important accounts, and monitor your financial accounts for unauthorized activity.

### What's the difference between a firewall and antivirus?
A firewall monitors and controls network traffic — it decides what data is allowed in and out of your network. Antivirus software scans files and programs on a device for known malware signatures and suspicious behavior. You need both: a firewall (your router has a built-in one) to protect the network perimeter, and antivirus on each device to catch threats that get through.

### How do I protect my children's devices on the network?
Use parental controls built into your router or DNS service (NextDNS and Cloudflare 1.1.1.3 offer content filtering), create a separate WiFi network for children's devices, install security software with parental monitoring capabilities (Norton Family, Kaspersky Safe Kids), enable SafeSearch on search engines, and have regular conversations about phishing, social engineering, and online safety.

---

## Conclusion: Your Monthly Security Routine

Securing your home network doesn't require a cybersecurity degree or an expensive enterprise setup. It requires **consistency**. Here's the approach that works:

**Today (one-time setup, ~2 hours):**
1. Secure your router (Step 1)
2. Configure WPA3 and guest network (Step 2)
3. Set up DNS-level protection with Cloudflare 1.1.1.3 or NextDNS (Step 4)
4. Install a password manager and migrate your passwords (Step 6)
5. Enable 2FA on your top 10 accounts (Step 7)

**This weekend (extended setup, ~2 hours):**
6. Set up router-level VPN if desired (Step 3)
7. Segment IoT devices onto a separate network (Step 5)
8. Install network monitoring (Step 8)
9. Verify antivirus is active on all devices (Step 9)

**Every month (30 minutes):**
10. Run the monthly checklist from Step 10

The uncomfortable truth is the same in 2026 as it was a decade ago — the vast majority of successful home network attacks exploit **default passwords, outdated firmware, and reused credentials**. The 10 steps in this guide address all three. A home network running up-to-date firmware, WPA3 encryption, a DNS-level blocker, and unique passwords behind 2FA is orders of magnitude harder to compromise than 90% of the networks out there.

You don't need to be unhackable. You just need to be harder to hack than the next house on the block.

---

*Last updated: March 3, 2026. Statistics sourced from Cybersecurity Ventures, FBI IC3 Annual Report, IoT security research by Forescout, AV-TEST Institute, and DeXpose IoT Hacking Statistics 2026. Product pricing verified at time of publication and subject to change. DNS and VPN recommendations based on independent testing by Tom's Guide, TechRadar, CyberInsider, and State of Surveillance encrypted DNS comparison.*

Total cost: $0. This covers about 85% of what you need.

mainEntity.name: How often should I change my WiFi password?

acceptedAnswer.text: Change your WiFi password quarterly, or immediately if you suspect unauthorized access, when a household member moves out, after a repair person or guest has had access, or if you've shared the password widely. Always use a strong, unique password stored in your password manager.

mainEntity.name: Are smart home devices safe to use?

acceptedAnswer.text: Smart home devices can be used safely with proper precautions: place them on a separate network (VLAN or guest WiFi), change default passwords, keep firmware updated, and disable unnecessary features like microphones and remote access. The biggest risk comes from devices that no longer receive manufacturer updates — replace any end-of-life smart device.

mainEntity.name: What should I do if I think my network has been compromised?

acceptedAnswer.text: Immediately disconnect from the internet (unplug router). Then factory reset your router, set new admin credentials, change your WiFi password, update firmware, scan all devices with antivirus software, change passwords on critical accounts from a different network (mobile data), enable 2FA on all important accounts, and monitor your financial accounts for unauthorized activity.

mainEntity.name: What's the difference between a firewall and antivirus?

acceptedAnswer.text: A firewall monitors and controls network traffic — it decides what data is allowed in and out of your network. Antivirus software scans files and programs on a device for known malware signatures and suspicious behavior. You need both: a firewall (your router has a built-in one) to protect the network perimeter, and antivirus on each device to catch threats that get through.

mainEntity.name: How do I protect my children's devices on the network?

acceptedAnswer.text: Use parental controls built into your router or DNS service (NextDNS and Cloudflare 1.1.1.3 offer content filtering), create a separate WiFi network for children's devices, install security software with parental monitoring capabilities, enable SafeSearch on search engines, and have regular conversations about phishing, social engineering, and online safety.
